data.title: Equinor Best-Positioned Company As Europe Fails To Adequately Build Natural Gas Inventories
data.description:
Investment thesis: With the first two months of 2025, namely April & May, where Europe historically expects to see a build in natural gas inventories behind us, we see a failure to close the gap relative to the average five-year storage levels. This makes it unlikely that storage levels will reach the five-year average by the time heating season starts in October, much less the 83% of capacity levels mandated by the EU in the post-2022 environment, defined by Europe's break with Russian energy. Equinor (NYSE:EQNR), Europe's largest natural gas provider, is well-positioned to benefit, as its natural gas output is expected to grow, even as Europe's supply prospects are seemingly becoming more dire, meaning that higher natural gas prices are to be expected in the future. Based on the latest European natural gas storage data, I decided to add to my existing position in Equinor shares recently, despite the lack of a pullback in its share price, which is typically when I tend to invest in any stock.

Shifting from buying on the dips to buying before a fundamentals-based rally.

The last time I covered Equinor, I was focused more on its share price decline in the past year, which made it an interesting investment prospect. Then, as now, I think its share price could potentially double from here, on higher European natural gas prices, combined with internal factors & decisions, for its shares to reach new all-time highs. The one major difference is that if my investment thesis proves correct, the window of opportunity to buy at current share price levels may be closing in the next few months, which is why I added to my current position in the last few days, even though its share price has been appreciating. I typically tend to look for a dip in a company's share price before buying.

Equinor's financial results are stable despite lower oil prices Y-O-Y.

Net earnings for the first quarter of this year came in at $2.63 billion, more or less matching the first quarter of 2024.

Operating income improved by 16% for the same period, suggesting that Equinor has some in-built resilience in the face of lower oil prices. It should be mentioned that Brent crude averaged about $82/barrel in Q1, 2024, and $73/barrel in the first quarter of this year.

As we can see, more than half of Equinor's upstream output is natural gas, a lot of it in Norway, which is sold on the European market. Europe's natural gas spot price was trending significantly higher in Q1, 2025 compared with the same period from the prior year. This probably helped to offset lower oil prices.

Another interesting development that probably helped to shore up earnings is the company's electricity generation segment.

As we can see, Equinor's renewable power segment saw a slight decline, meaning that it is no longer investing in expanding its green energy portfolio. Gas power generation saw a sizable increase. To get a sense of the effect this shift in the company's power generation by source has on its earnings, it is worth looking at the company's 2024 earnings by segment.

Equinor's renewable power generation segment was its only negative operating earnings segment in both 2023 & 2024. The shift in policy helps to limit those financial losses, which we are likely to gradually see show up in yearly financial reports, including in the current year.

Other financial results of note for the latest available quarter include a 19% increase in revenues to over $29.9 billion. Total finance debt increased from $19.4 billion in Q1, 2024 to $20.5 billion for the latest quarter. Equinor has been steadily allocating roughly $500-$550 million per quarter to share buybacks. It is forecasting a 4% increase in upstream oil & gas production for this year, reversing last year's trend of a slight decline in production. If European natural gas prices go in the direction that current fundamentals suggest, the higher production volume comes at an opportune time for Equinor.

Europe's long path toward a structural, near-permanent gas crisis.

Looking at the trend-setting Dutch TTF natural gas spot price, we can observe that something changed for the European gas market in 2021, months before the Ukraine war went into full open conflict mode in early 2022.

The price spike, predating the cut-off of most Russian imports, was triggered by a shortfall in wind power generation in 2021, which led to a significant increase in natural gas as a backup source. That event highlighted the rising importance of weather patterns as a variable factor in the European energy market.

The 2022 breakup of EU-Russia economic relations cemented the concept of Europe being in a new era in terms of energy security and price trends post-2021.

The chart shows a dramatic drop in EU natural gas imports starting in 2022, as Russian gas imports were slashed by about 75%. It is not as obvious in the chart, but the first quarter of this year saw a further decline in Russian imports from about 13.5 Bcm/quarter last year to about 10 Bcm/quarter currently, largely due to the end of Russian gas transit through Ukraine.

In inverse correlation to the EU's pivot away from Russian gas, US gas exports to Europe saw a dramatic increase. First, following the 2022 crisis, then in the first quarter of this year, US LNG exports to the EU reached 18.4 Bcm, up from 16 Bcm in Q1 2024. For reference, in Q1 2021, US LNG exports to Europe amounted to only 4 Bcm.

Most of America's LNG exports are now going to Europe, yet it does not seem to be enough. It should be noted that other major sources of imports, like Norway & Azerbaijan, also saw a decline in exports for the quarter. Flying under the market's radar is the fact that Norway, which is currently the EU's largest source of natural gas, is experiencing a decline in output. This year's production will be about 6 Bcm lower compared with last year, with future years mostly expected to see a gradual worsening of the situation.

It should also be mentioned that the US, the main replacement for Russian gas, is seeing a stagnation in natural gas production since late 2023. The EU is currently working on a plan to eliminate Russian gas imports as well as other sources of Russian energy by the end of 2027. My take is that at least those EU officials who have a clear understanding of the overall EU energy market and its broader prospects may be banking on countries that oppose the EU plan, such as Hungary or Slovakia, to veto it.

Contrary to rhetoric, the EU does not have a solid energy market situation that can absorb another shock that would result from losing the rest of the Russian natural gas it still buys. Maintaining the current levels of non-Russian supplies looks precarious, with major suppliers such as Norway and the US not in a position to dramatically increase exports. I do not believe that there will be a total ban on Russian energy by the end of 2027, but as is often the case, politics and the trappings of one's past rhetoric will trump economic realities. I do believe that regardless of what happens next with the Ukraine war, Russian natural gas supplies to the EU will be further reduced. In conclusion, aside from brief periods of favorable weather, where winters will be mild, the wind will be steadily blowing and the sun shining, Europe's natural gas market will be under constant supply strain conditions for the foreseeable future.

Investment implications.

Equinor is currently Europe's largest supplier of natural gas, thus, it is well-positioned to take advantage of what is shaping up to be a period of long-term higher natural gas prices in Europe, which will only be punctuated by weather-related price drops. In terms of valuation, it is currently trading at a P/E ratio of just over 7, which is much lower compared with fellow European oil major peers such as Shell (SHEL) and BP (BP) that I recently covered.

Shell is currently trading at a P/E ratio of about 10.6, while BP is trading at 11.7. Shell may arguably have upside potential on the back of its robust LNG segment, but BP, the European peer that is trading at the highest valuation level, does not have any such obvious potential upside catalysts. With a dividend of almost 9%, based on historical payouts, it is also far more attractive compared with its above-mentioned European peers in this regard as well.

Equinor is currently my largest non-Canadian holding in the oil & gas segment of my portfolio. I started buying shares in November of last year when I saw that the European natural gas market was in a prolonged supply deficit situation.

Even though the European market tightened significantly since then, as I expected it to, Equinor's share price did not see a corresponding gain. I have been buying incrementally since then, adding to my position on dips. My latest purchase of Equinor shares is not in response to a dip, but rather a recognition of the fact that the fundamentals, particularly when it comes to Europe's natural gas market, are likely to kick in soon. It will drive European natural gas prices higher and Equinor's stock price with it.

  • Risks to my thesis.

Several factors could derail what increasingly appears to be a prelude to a sustained European natural gas price rally. The weather is the number one unknown and unpredictable factor. European natural gas demand could potentially decline on favorable weather, and it could do so for years. For instance, many successive years of warmer-than-normal winters could do it.

An unexpected event that might lead to an EU-Russia rapprochement looks increasingly unlikely, but it is not impossible, and if that happens, Russia could still use existing pipeline facilities to immediately increase natural gas supplies to Europe. Europe's economy could slow more than it already has, leading to a decline in demand. Chinese demand for LNG could potentially decline on higher pipeline imports from Russia and Central Asia, combined with slower economic growth, which could flood the European market with plenty of LNG supplies. There are also potentially unforeseen company-specific risks that need to be considered, such as a decline in the company's upstream profitability or an industrial accident that could temporarily impact its share price.

  • Potential upside makes it worth the wait.

Given how the European natural gas storage levels evolved since I first invested in Equinor shares over half a year ago, my expectations were for this stock to rise significantly by now. However, despite the deteriorated natural gas storage situation, which shows no signs of improvement, the European natural gas market remained remarkably tame in the past months. It is a classical case of fundamentals being temporarily thwarted by headlines, where stories of more US LNG going to the EU, or the EU lowering storage fill capacity targets seen as reasons to be dovish on European natural gas prices. It would not be the first time that rhetoric shapes the market to a greater degree than fundamentals, at least in the short term.

Fundamentals do win out eventually, especially when they are not transitory but rather structural, as it seems to be the case with European natural gas supply prospects. With every day that passes, as we get closer to Fall & Winter, without the gap between actual storage levels and the historical average being closed down, the downside for Equinor's share price declines, and its potential for upside increases.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Palantir: Sell The Euphoria (Technical Analysis, Downgrade)
data.description:
Intro

In my previous article, I found that Palantir Technologies Inc. (NASDAQ:PLTR) had strong technicals and fundamentals but was overvalued, leading to a hold rating. In the below analysis, while much of this is still true, I find that near term technical strength has receded and more importantly, negative divergence at the all-time high signals a potential incoming bearish reversal. For the fundamentals, Q1 earnings were impressive and guidance for both Q2 and the full year show that the outlook is bright. Still, the P/S ratio is at nose-bleed levels as growth is unable to keep up with the multiple's expansion. Therefore, I downgrade Palantir to a sell rating as the valuation is unattractive and the technicals show that the top is likely in.

Technicals

Charting

Palantir was in an uptrend that started at the early April lows but recently a pullback has broken this trend line. That doesn't rule out further gains but horizontal drift or a bearish reversal are also possibilities now. For support, the nearest three levels are identified above. The first is at about 120 since this level was support during the previous four trading days. Moving down, 107 is likely to be an important area as it was briefly resistance in late February as the stock attempted to rebound from a downside gap and was support in early May. The last support level identified here is at 99, very near the key round number 100. This area was support after the gap up in early February and was resistance in late March and mid April. As for resistance, the stock is quite near all-time highs and so there is only one other key level in addition to the former uptrend line. It is at 125.2 just above the current stock price. This level was key resistance in February and in early May. Overall, while the uptrend line is broken, I would still consider this chart to be slightly positive as the relatively abundant amount of support should prevent quickly losses from here.

Moving Averages

The 50 day SMA has been above the 200 day SMA for the past six months and so clearly the long term has been consistently bullish for Palantir. The stock was able to break back above the 50 day SMA as a sign of strength in early to mid April and since early May, the gap between the SMAs has been expanding. This shows that long term upward momentum is accelerating. For the EMAs, the 9 day line broke above the 21 day line back in mid April as a bullish near term signal. However, recently the gap between the EMAs has decreased and so near term momentum has slowed considerably. Both EMAs are quite near the stock currently and may be important support moving forward. The 21 day EMA was key support in early May. For the Bollinger Bands, the stock broke above the midline in early to mid April to show that an uptrend may be beginning to form. It then confirmed the trend as it acted as support in late April. While the uptrend line was broken recently, the stock has remained above the midline and so therefore, gains are still possible but perhaps at a slower pace. As a whole, these moving averages remain positive in my view but near term momentum has likely peaked.

Indicators

A bullish crossover occurred in the MACD back in early April. Earlier this month, the MACD line was able to bounce off the signal line as a sign of bullish resilience but a bearish crossover has since occurred. Hope is not completely lost for the bulls, however, as the histogram bar on the current trading day has turned green. Relative to the long term, there are concerning developments in the MACD. Note that the indicator was far from surpassing its February highs when the stock made fresh all-time highs just recently. This is negative divergence that shows this rally is potentially on weak legs. For the RSI, a bit of a downtrend has been in effect since the reading of over 70 at the start of May. Still, the current reading of 59.93 shows that the bulls are in control by a decent amount. Like the MACD, the RSI exhibits bearish divergence as its high this month wasn't anywhere close to its February peak. Lastly, for the stochastics, a bearish crossover occurred not long ago but now the %K and %D are nearly inseparable. Therefore, there is some uncertainty currently. Both lines have dropped out of the 80+ zone and so clearly sentiment is no longer as bullish. For the near term, I believe it's clear that peak momentum is behind us but the indicators show there's some remaining strength in the stock. For the long term, negative divergence in the MACD and RSI shows the potential for a top and also a bearish reversal.

Fibonacci Fan Lines

Using the early April low and the all-time high set earlier this month, Fibonacci Fan Lines are shown on the above chart. As Palantir pulled back, it has broken beneath the 23.6% fan line as a sign of weakness. Despite dipping further after that, the stock remains above the 38.2% fan line and seems to have found some support at that line in the past few days. Still, the 38.2% fan line is rising quickly and so the stock will have to rally soon to avoid another breakdown. The 50% fan line is relatively distant currently, but investors should be cautious as a break below that line would further increase the odds of a bearish reversal.

Fundamentals

Earnings

Quarterly Results

Palantir reported impressive 2025 Q1 earnings back on May 5th in my view. The company generated total revenues of $884 million, up 39% YoY. This beat consensus expectations by a healthy margin of $21.72 million, showing strong business activity. As you can see in the chart above, revenue growth accelerated QoQ and set a fresh multiyear high. In addition, U.S. commercial revenue growth of 71% YoY to $255 million clearly shows that the diversification efforts are working out really well. Total customer count also grew by an impressive 39% YoY showing that the increase in revenues is more than just existing customers spending more. They are successfully attracting new clients. For EPS, Palantir posted an adjusted figure of $0.13, up from $0.08 in the prior year period. This was in-line with analyst estimates. Operating cash flows were also healthy for the company as they reported $310 million, representing a 139.4% growth rate. However, as you can see above, that is only around average despite a QoQ acceleration. Lastly, adjusted operating margin increased from 36% to 44% YoY and so clearly the company's efficiency is on a good direction.

Guidance

For their second quarter, Palantir is expecting to see revenues in the range of $934 million to $938 million. The midpoint of this guidance would imply growth of 38% from the year ago quarter's $678 million figure. Therefore, growth is expected to be relatively unchanged from Q1 and will remain at far above average levels. They are also projecting adjusted operating income of between $401 million and $405 million. Midpoint YoY growth would therefore be 59%. In their Q1, they saw 73% growth for this figure and so this guidance does represent some slowdown. For the full year 2025, Palantir raised their guidance for multiple key metrics including U.S. commercial revenue and adjusted free cash flow and so overall, the outlook for the company is very strong at this point.

Valuation

Price To Sales Ratio [P/S]

There has been some whipsawing in the P/S ratio this year. The multiple surged to an above 100 reading then plunged to 60. It has now recovered to 99.89 and despite the strong fundamentals, I believe this ratio now shows significant overvaluation. As discussed above, revenue growth has accelerated to multiyear highs and their U.S. commercial business is firing on all cylinders. Q2 guidance shows that impressive revenue growth will continue even as the trend of growth acceleration is expected to end. Furthermore, the company raising their guidance for the full year shows that the longer term outlook is no less impressive. However, the Information Technology sector's median P/S ratio is only 2.98 and Palantir is therefore at a 3252% premium. A growth rate in the high 30s percentage range is definitely no slouch but for this kind of a price tag, you would need significantly more growth for the valuation to be justified. While the multiple deserves to be at an above average state, it seems to have expanded far out of proportion relative to the improvements in the fundamentals. It is normal to pay a premium price for a premium business but personally, I believe this valuation now signals euphoria.

Conclusion

While many technical indications remain net positive, I believe there is now a strong risk of a significant down move. Near term bullish momentum has receded considerably and the negative divergence signals in the MACD and RSI show that the top is likely in for now and that there's a potential for a bearish reversal. For the fundamentals, there is not much to complain about in their Q1 earnings and guidance for the rest of the year. However, as discussed above, the P/S ratio has expanded out of proportion and seems disconnected with reality even as the fundamentals continue to strengthen. In my previous article, I reiterated my hold rating as the unattractive valuation was offset by the bullish technicals. This time around, the appearance of topping signals make Palantir a sell in my view.

data.title: Oshkosh Corporation: Buy This Undervalued Tactical Vehicle Leader
data.description:
Oshkosh Corporation (NYSE:OSK) is a leading manufacturer of purpose-built trucks, defense-grade vehicles and machinery. While not a pure-play defense stock, Oshkosh plays a pivotal role in supplying tactical wheeled vehicles to the U.S. Army and allied militaries. In this report, I analyze how the company’s defense segment — including programs such as the Joint Light Tactical Vehicle [JLTV] — provides a strong foundation for long-term value creation, underpinning my Buy rating.

Oshkosh Corporation: From Lifting Equipment To Tactical Vehicles

Oshkosh Corporation operates through three segments and each of those segments has its own products and market dynamics. The Access segment produces Aerial Work Platforms or AWPs and Telehandlers. The Vocational segment includes fire, refuse, concrete and municipal vehicles. The defense portfolio includes Joint Light Tactical Vehicles, which is the successor to the Humvee. It also includes the family of medium tactical vehicles, heavy expanded mobility tactical trucks and the palletized load system. Somewhat interesting is that the Defense segment also includes the Next Generation Delivery Vehicle used by the United States Postal Service. The defense portfolio also includes the Medium Caliber Weapon System and robotic vehicles.

What I like about the company is the diverse product portfolio, the diversity in the end market and its defense portfolio ranges from light vehicles to heavy vehicles and also includes autonomous solutions which I believe are becoming increasingly more important in all markets. The company is also ramping up its production for the delivery vehicles which should be fully ramped by year-end and provide significant growth this year and next year. Furthermore, there are aftermarket revenue opportunities as well.

Oshkosh Corporation Q1 2025 Earnings Decline

While I am upbeat on the company’s prospects, first quarter sales showing a high single digit decline to $2.3 billion. Access sales declined almost 23% to $957.1 million while Defense sales declined by 9.1% to $463 million despite $50 million in delivery vehicles added to the defense sales mix. Vocational sales were the highlight with sales growing 12% to $866.8 million driven by strong pricing.

The Access sales declined primarily due to softer market conditions in North America while the defense sales were also affected by lower volumes and catch-up adjustments. Operating income declined 30.3% to $191.8 million indicating a margin contraction of 2.5 percentage points to 8.3%. So, overall sales were down due to lower Access and Defense volumes and adjustments, partially offset by improved Vocational pricing and profits declined due the lower sales volume, spent on new product development, higher operating expenses and a $17.5 million headwind on profit adjustments according to the EAC principle.

Oshkosh Corporation Faces Tariff Headwind

The tariff and trade environment remains fluid, so what the company announced in April during the earnings call may already no longer be valid but it is important to keep the risk of tariffs in mind. The company expects $11 in earnings per share for the full year, but there is a $1 headwind from tariffs that will partially be offset by cost reductions. Essentially, this would indicate an EPS guidance of $10.50. For companies that have a globally oriented supply chain tariffs remain a cost burden and the company’s guidance clearly shows this.

Oshkosh Corporation Has An Attractive Valuation

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

The compounded annual growth rate on EBITDA is not necessarily attractive at 3%. However, the improving free cash flow margins definitely are attractive. Driven by solid demand for defense equipment and the next generation delivery vehicle, which is going through ramp up and learning curve there should be significant free cash flow growth ahead outpacing EBITDA growth. Furthermore, the company has a low leverage ticking another checkbox for a buy rating. As a result, I am marking the stock a buy with a $158.67 price target in line with the company’s median EV/EBITDA which is lower than that of the peer group.

Conclusion: Oshkosh Corporation Stock Is Looking Attractive

I believe that Oshkosh Corporation stock is attractive. The company does face some tariff impact, but we see healthy demand for defense vehicles, a ramp up in the next generation delivery vehicle for the USPS and improved pricing on Vocational vehicles. The company certainly has to keep an eye on any added tariff impacts and cost efficiency on defense programs, but overall I do believe that at current pricing the stock provides compelling upside.

data.title: Japanese Vs. U.S. Bond Markets: Which Are More Cooked?
data.description:
No one wants Japan’s sovereign debt.

The global sovereign bond collapse appears to be rapidly worsening. The Bank of Japan (BoJ) owns more than half of its own government bonds, desperate to prop up the economy by buying domestic debt that nobody else wants. But no central bank can prop up an economy forever.

Japan has a long-running fiscal problem which has only gotten worse, ending its epic run of negative interest rate policy in 2024. It seems that fewer and fewer people are interested in holding US debt, but this year’s bond selloff is extending to other countries as well, including Japan.

Japan has no good options after over a decade of aggressive monetary easing, including quantitative easing and yield curve control, aimed at combating deflation and stimulating growth. By purchasing Japanese government bonds, the BoJ kept yields artificially low, enabling the government to finance its staggering debt at minimal cost. However, as inflation in Japan rises, the BoJ is faced with a delicate balancing act: “normalizing” policy without triggering a fiscal crisis in a heavily indebted economy. Now, sovereign bond markets all around the world are showing serious signs of trouble.

The yen carry trade used to be a reliable bet because investors could borrow zero-interest “safe haven” yen to invest in other currencies that paid higher yields. But it depends on both the yen and the other currency, like the dollar, to be within a certain range of strength relative to one another. It requires knowledge of which currency is weaker, and which side’s central bankers are going to keep rates lower or higher.

With higher interest rates in Japan and Japanese bond yields surging, it could unwind the yen carry trade and push surging US Treasury yields even higher. This all after Moody’s downgraded the US credit rating.

At the end of the day, both the dollar and the yen are locked into a slow-motion race to the bottom. Collapsing bonds are among the most glaring symptoms of an oncoming global economic crash. Japan can’t afford sky-high long-term yields, with an out of control debt to GDP ratio, slow growth, and other worrying factors.

The ripple effects are significant for US Treasurys, where Japan is the largest foreign holder with over $1 trillion in holdings. As Japanese government bond yields become more attractive, Japanese investors, including banks and pension funds, are repatriating capital, reducing demand for U.S. bonds. This divestment could push U.S. Treasury yields higher, with the 10-year and 30-year yields already climbing to alarming levels.

10-Year Treasury Yields, Mid-2020 to Present

Higher U.S. yields increase borrowing costs for the U.S. government, already grappling with a swelling deficit, and could force the Federal Reserve to reconsider rate cuts if inflation spikes. Bessent says we can “outgrow” the debt, but there’s no chance.

Elevated yields could choke economic growth by increasing mortgage, credit card, and auto loan rates, squeezing consumers and businesses already rocked by high prices and excessive debt. A sharp sell-off might destabilize financial markets, as seen in recent volatility attributed to “bond vigilantes” protesting fiscal policy.

Worse, if foreign demand for US Treasurys dries up, especially from Japan or China, the U.S. could face a funding crisis. That’s when quantitative easing will have to enter the picture, worsening inflation in an attempt to finance surging deficits and kick the collapse can down the road.

Japan’s bond market turmoil is a stark reminder: global financial interconnectedness means no market is an island, and bond markets in both the US and Japan are screaming loud and clear. The US will be forced into QE one way or another, fueling an inflationary beast that the Fed has never really had under control.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Intellia: Heart Disease Safety Setback Triggers Selloff - Caution Advised
data.description:
Investment Overview

Intellia Therapeutics, Inc. (NASDAQ:NTLA) shares are in free-fall in trading so far today, down >25%, after the gene therapy specialist disclosed in a regulatory filing released post-market yesterday that a patient involved in its global Phase 3 MAGNITUDE study of candidate nexiguran ziclumeran ("nex-z"), indicated for the heart disorder ATTR-CM (transthyretin amyloid cardiomyopathy), experienced elevated liver enzymes.

Specifically, the company noted:

There has been a single, recent, asymptomatic patient with grade 4 liver transaminase elevations based on laboratory tests, which appear to be resolving without hospitalization or medical intervention and have fallen to grade 3 ALT and grade 2 AST elevations. We continue to monitor these events as the MAGNITUDE study progresses.

Intellia notes in the same filing that:

enrollment in our global Phase 3 MAGNITUDE study of nex-z for ATTR-CM is progressing according to our expectations. Currently, we have enrolled approximately 365 patients in the MAGNITUDE study, out of an expected total enrollment of approximately 765 patients, and anticipate enrollment completion by early 2027.

Intellia - Background To Today's News & Explaining The Selloff

Intellia - a gene therapy specialist focused on developing CRISPR/Cas9 technology (see my previous note on the company for a detailed explanation of this approach) - first entered into a license and collaboration agreement with Regeneron Pharmaceuticals (REGN), the Watertown, Massachusetts based Pharma giant, in 2016.

The two companies are currently partnered on two clinical stage programs, and various pre-clinical. The later stage program is nex-z, led by Intellia, in Transthyretin Amyloidosis ("ATTR"), which is at the Phase 3 clinical study stage in ATTR with cardiomyopathy.

In a recent investor presentation, Intellia states that:

CM patients have debilitating shortness of breath, arrythmias, reduced mobility and quality of life, as well as a high rate of hospitalization

And also notes:

  • In phase 3 studies of silencer or stabilizer therapies for CM, the annual rate of CV events or death is high at ~15% of enrolled patients in the first year
  • Even on existing therapies, CM patients have a marked decline in quality of life and functional capacity as measured by 6MWT (6 minute walk test).

Intellia's candidate is somewhat revolutionary in that it may offer a "one-and-done" therapy with potential to provide life-long reduction in disease burden, and lie-long freedom from chronic therapy, i.e., multiple injections / hundreds of pills per annum. Below is an overview of the MAGNITUDE study from a recent Intellia corporate presentation:

Intellia says its Phase 1 clinical results for nex-z showed "potential to be best TTR-directed drug," with "deep, rapid, consistent and durable reductions in serum TTR," superior to vutrisiran, an RNA-interference drug developed by Alnylam Pharma (ALNY), approved in June 2022 to treat polyneuropathy of hereditary transthyretin-mediated (hATTR) amyloidosis in adults, with the drug's label being expanded to include ATTR-CM patients in March this year.

The current best-selling ATTR-CM drug is Pfizer's (PFE) Vyndaqel, which earned $5.4bn of revenues in 2024, while BridgeBio's (BBIO) Attruby was also approved in November last year.

Still, Intellia's therapy has major advantages in terms of dosing, as discussed above, while management says Phase 1 data (shown in the slide below) demonstrates clear superiority to vutrisiran and acoramidis (Attruby) - with some significant caveats.

The data has been weighted "to match the genotype distribution from the HELIOS-B trial," and Intellia additionally notes "cross-trial comparisons have inherent limitations and should be interpreted with caution."

On the safety side, Intellia reported that in the Phase 1 study, nex-z was "well tolerated," but an instance of elevated liver enzymes can drastically undermine a drug's progress - witness Pfizer's recent shelving of its GLP-1 receptor agonist weight loss drug danuglipron, owing to a single instance of "drug-induced liver injury which resolved after discontinuation of danuglipron."

Elevated liver enzymes can lead to serious liver conditions such as hepatitis, cirrhosis, or even liver cancer, and it is therefore possible that, even if nex-z aced its Phase studies from an efficacy perspective, the drug could be refused approval.

Is The Market Overreacting To A Single Adverse Safety Event

We may speculate that if the patient in question's issues resolved quickly, and moved from Grade 4, to Grade 3 / Grade 2, then it's possible today's sell-off has been overdone.

That seems to be the view of several Wall Street analysts, who have apparently suggested the selloff was disproportionate to the safety setback recorded, and that it reflects market jitters, especially given separate issues suffered by the likes of Rocket Pharmaceuticals (RCKT) (a clinical hold was placed on a Phase 2 study of RP-A501 after a patient experienced a serious adverse event), and Sarepta Therapeutics (SRPT) (a patient death was reported related to use of its Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy gene therapy drug).

The change in leadership at the Department for Health and Human Services ("HHS"), with Robert Kennedy appointed, at the FDA, Dr. Marty Makary has replaced Peter Marks, and at the Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research ("CBER"), where Dr Vinay Prasad has assumed leadership, has also created uncertainty around prospects for gene therapies.

Dr Prasad, for example has been critical in the past of the FDA's accelerated approval process, which is how many gene therapies have secured approval in the past (essentially, drugs are approved based on a biomarker indicative of positive treatment effect, and a post-confirmatory study initiated to confirm the drug's disease modifying qualities).

On a more positive note, while Pfizer shelved development of Danuglipron based on a single case of elevated enzyme's, my understanding is that that patient's issues resolved after discontinuation of the drug, while the nex-z patient's issue resolved without "medical intervention" - between days 28 and 36, the patients' ALT and AST elevations had fallen back to grade 3 and grade 2 status naturally.

There are also questions about what may be causing the nex-z safety issue. For example, I recently covered Verve Therapeutics (VERV) and its attempts to treat types of Atherosclerotic Cardiovascular Disease ("ASCVD"), and like Intellia, Verve uses lipid nano particles ("LNPs") to deliver its gene therapy.

Verve has experienced adverse events itself (it uses base editing as opposed to CRISPR for gene-edits), and has determined they were related to the LNP it had been using. Now, Intellia may need to complete similar research, to determine if the therapy, or the LNP, are at fault, which could be costly and cause delays.

What Is The Outlook Going Forward?

Nex-z is not Intellia's only candidate - in fact, NTLA-2002, targeting herediray angioedema, is also at the Phase 3 study stage. This is a wholly-owned asset, therefore arguably more valuable to Intellia than nex-z in terms of future revenue generation.

In its Phase 2 studies of NTLA-2002, 11 of 11 patients experienced reduction in HAE attacks (the condition causes potentially life-threatening swelling attacks), and in Phase 1 studies, durability of at least 2 years was established. Like nex-z, Intellia believes it has a "best-in-class" candidate that could become "standard-of-care" in a multi-billion dollar market. Regeneron is taking the lead on a hemophilia B drug, also, and there are other research projects ongoing.

Nevertheless, the safety issues continue to weigh heavily. Patients may be understandably reluctant to undergo a therapy that makes permanent changes to their DNA, no matter what clinical study results suggest - we should note that the long-term effects of such treatments remain unknown, and that once commercialized, patients won't necessarily be monitored for years afterwards as clinical study participants are.

It's also possible that nex-z may be most effective used in conjunction with, e.g., Vyndaqel, which could be problematic from an insurance reimbursement perspective - would insurers be prepared to pay for two separate therapies?

Intellia has countered that Vyndaqel generics will be on the market by the time of any nex-z commercial launch, reducing their cost, but that does beg the question - is nex-z a truly "one-and-done" therapy, or might it not be as effective as it may need to be to secure approval?

Concluding Thoughts - Is Today's Selloff In NTLA On Adverse Safety Data A "Buy-The-Dip" Moment

Intellia's current market cap is $738m, whereas, when the company first demonstrated that NTLA-2001, the predecessor to NTLA-2002, was able to reduce serum TTR in patients when delivered in-vivo, it represented an historic first for gene therapies, and drove Intellia's market cap >$10bn.

Unfortunately, progress has been slow and the market has become increasingly skeptical of the "one-and-done" approach since then, despite its obvious advantages, as demonstrated by today's selloff.

The ATTR-CM space is a huge market that supports multi-billion revenue potential for perhaps 4-5 therapies, so investors should not be dismissive of Intellia's prospects, despite today's events and the overriding backdrop, in my view. We may speculate that HAE offers up a chance to generate "blockbuster" (>$1bn per annum) sales, also.

Clearly, to grow its valuation, Intellia must secure approval for one of its assets - the company has racked up an accumulated deficit of ~$2.3bn, and has ~$700m of cash and near-term at its disposal, against a net loss of $(114m) in Q1 2025, versus $(107m) in the prior year.

This feels like a key moment in the company's progression, as if NTLA-2002 or nex-z fails to secure approval, I am unsure if investor's would have the patience to continue to fund heavy losses and the development of a next-generation drug, attempting to avoid the issues of the prior version.

The bear thesis is that so long as there is uncertainty on safety, Intellia's drugs are unapprovable, and cash will be swiftly burned through, leaving investors empty-handed. The bull thesis is that the patient's issues quickly resolved and that one or perhaps two drugs could therefore be approved within the next 2-3 years, and claim best-in-class status.

It's a tight call - and as such, I find myself on the sidelines.

data.title: How Ford Is Secretly Winning From Trump's Tariff
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The tariffs imposed by the Trump administration not only led to a new cost realignment for auto makers, but also shifted around the competitive landscape in the U.S. auto industry with specific charges on steel, aluminum, and auto parts imported from other countries. Nevertheless, Ford leverages its domestic production apparatus to take advantage that this obstacle presents for US auto makers, and gains a competitive edge in cost-effectiveness. The fair value of $14.63 puts Ford in a commanding position to bag an incredible upside of 41.2%.

Company Overview

In 1923 Ford, was founded in Dearborn, Michigan. The automaker designs and manufactures cars, trucks, and SUVs, including the famous Mustang cars and F-series trucks. It also supports the production of electric vehicles such as the Mustang Mach-E and autonomous driving technology. Additionally, Ford provides financial services through Ford Credit for vehicle financing.

The Tariff Edge

Trump's Tariff Overview

The automotive industry has been transformed due to the tariffs imposed by President Trump on materials and components sourced abroad, which has led to a considerable increase in the cost for manufacturers who rely heavily on imports. The import tariffs include 25% on steel and 10% on aluminum, which are essential materials for vehicle frames, bodies, and other components. There is an additional 2.5% tariff cost of importing auto parts like engines, transmissions, and electronics. The main objectives of these tariffs is to incentivize production in the US through punishing imports.

Supply Chain

Ford sourced nearly half of its production from U.S and Canada-based suppliers based on the chart provided by Kogod School of Business, which measures the average Total Domestic Content of popular car brands across all of their models. Even though Ford shows an increase in foreign sourcing exposure compared to a decade ago, it still will have a comparatively lower exposure to import tariffs relative to many other U.S. automakers. This would give Ford a vital advantage it must have over competitors like Volkswagen and Toyota.

Partnerships with American companies plays a very important role in executing on this advantage. For example, the joint venture with SK Innovation to build battery plants worth $11.4 billion in Kentucky and Tennessee is one of the major partnerships that helps keep the supply of electric vehicles (EVs) tariff-free. Also, Ford is sourcing 90% of its steel domestically, thus alleviating the effect of the 25% steel tariff. 75% of its aluminum is sourced domestically, thereby mitigating the 10% tariff. Ford uses 1 million tons of steel a year at the price of $800 per ton. With 80% being domestically sourced, Ford would only need to import 200,000 tons, which would come with a $40 million tariff cost. On the other hand, Toyota, which imports 40% of its steel and other materials from Aichi, would face a $100 million hit, which is considerably higher.

Manufacturing

The U.S. manufacturing network, which is Ford's pillar of success, also supports Ford's tariff resilience. In 2024, the automaker manufactured 2.5 million vehicles in the United States, accounting for 80% of its North American production. Major plants include the Dearborn Truck Plant in Michigan, which is equipped to produce 500,000 F-150 trucks yearly, the Kentucky Truck Plant that assembles 400,000 Super Duty trucks and SUVs, and its Wayne, Michigan Assembly Plant, which was upgraded with a $1.2 billion investment to manufacture 200,000 Broncos and Rangers. Ford's vertical integration—producing components such as engines and transmissions at in-house facilities like the Rouge Complex—eliminates the need for parts that are imported, which is why they don't have to pay the 2.5% parts tariff while improving cost control at the same time.

Lower cost burden

With a majority of its production capacity within the US, Ford has an average tariff of only $500 per vehicle, whereas Volkswagen, which imports 80% of its 900,000 U.S. vehicles, is facing a potential 2,000% on imported parts or units. Ford's 30% imported parts incur 2.5% of duty as compared to Toyota's or GM's ~50% of cars being built abroad. While Ford still expects a $2.5 billion impact and as a result suspended guidance for now, others are much more exposed. Ford's core advantages of localization, vertical integration, partnerships, and flexibility are obvious, evident through its $5.6 billion investment is in BlueOval City, a project in Tennessee that will integrate EV assembly and battery production. The project will insulate the company from tariffs on imported battery units as a result, and create 6,000 jobs.

USMCA

The USMCA (United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement) is a trade agreement that requires strict rules of origin to be followed. Under this Agreement, at least 3/4ths of total components of a vehicle must be North American-produced in order to achieve zero tariff status. The new rule represents an increase from the 62.5% limit that was established under NAFTA. Through this strategy, USMCA aims to reinforce local manufacturing and achieve self-reliance in the supply chain, thereby preventing American business reliance on foreign suppliers. Slight improvements in its supply chain will enable Ford to easily meet the USMCA requirement, thus securing tariff-free trade throughout the region. Compliance with this standard operationally saves Ford from import duties that were extorted on goods during President Trump's term which in turn helps Ford remain price-competitive in a market which is highly price-elastic. On the other hand, for companies like Toyota and Volkswagen which can only ensure a smaller percentage of local sourcing than Ford, the tariff situation is more complicated.

The labor value content (LVC) rule of the USMCA represents another point of differentiation for Ford, which is to require that 40-45% of auto content be manufactured by employees who are to be paid at least $16 per hour in an effort to achieve fair labor standards. Ford's strong U.S. manufacturing background and the presence of a high-wage workforce ensure that it attains the requirements with little effort, while its challengers in low-wage areas would need to cope with additional costs due to higher wages or shifting production. Through the incorporation of USMCA compliance provisions in the supply chain, Ford managed to mitigate the tariff impacts and at the same time gain a competitive edge.

The Competitive Scene

GM

Even though General Motors (GM) also sources a similar amount of its raw materials domestically GM has a marked vulnerability to the tariffs imposed by Trump since nearly half of the cars sold by GM in the U.S. happen to be imported, which is a huge difference compare to Ford's 20% imported vehicles. GM's situation is further emphasized by the fact that 750,000 cars accounted for its annual importation of vehicles on its critical brands such as Chevy Silverado and GMC Sierra.

Moreover, GM's operations have a particular downside regarding its Mexico plant along with its operations in China, complicating tariff related costs. For instance, due to the imposition of the 25% tariff, the profitability of the production of electric cars (EVs) such as the Equinox and Blazer SUVs in Mexico will be put at risk. A straightforward solution like the relocation of production to the U.S. can be rather expensive and time-consuming, thus making it hard for GM to be flexible in its strategy.

Ford, on the other hand, operates more effectively. Since 80% of the cars that are sold by it in the US are made in the local plants, Ford is not that much exposed to as much of these vehicle import tariffs. Focusing on manufacturing more of the high-margin trucks and SUVs which includes products like the F-150 is a well-thought-out strategy. In addition, Ford in advance has introduced discounts to align current prices to previous, lower vehicle prices which had been hiked due to tariffs, aggressively using the advantage of local production.

Toyota

Toyota produces 1.9 million vehicles annually in North America but also brings in 500,000 from Japan, which accounts for 24% of its U.S. sales out of 2.1 million total sales. Conversely, 80% of the cars sold in the US by Ford are made in the US. The USMCA provides duty-free trade for North American-made vehicles meeting the regional content requirement, whereby both companies are the direct beneficiaries. However, Toyota's reliance on imports from Japan is a risk with the potential tariff of 25%, whereas Ford's insignificant non-North American imports mitigate its exposure.

Financially, a 25% tariff on imported cars with an average unit value of $30,000 is $7,500 per vehicle. In line with this, it would cost Toyota $3.75 billion each year due to this tariff on 500,000 cars. Ford's 100,000 imports would be liable for $750 million comparatively. This five-fold difference causes a huge strain on Toyota’s profitability, as the tariff could even eliminate margins completely on the estimated $2,000 profit per vehicle. Ford’s strong financial position is sustained through less exposure.

The DCF Model

The WACC of 11.09% in my calculation is line with Aswath Damodaran's Auto industry's WACC of 10.34%. This broadly highlights the higher cost of debt that companies in this industry have to incur. This implies that lenders demand higher yields due to the higher perceived higher risk.

Ford presents a substantial growth opportunity of 41.2% to investors. The model deploys fairly conservative projections, albeit in line with the median financial performances of the automaker, to deduce the fair value of $14.63. This effectively indicates Ford’s earnings potential and its leverage to capture market share amidst the paradigm shift the US automobile industry currently going through.

Key Risks

For Ford Motor Company, the U.S. economic predicament represents a major risk through a number of elements. The economy's demand is threatened as its growth is further slowing, with GDP projected at 1.5% for 2025. This also indirectly affects the consumer demand for vehicles.

This table of last quarter's financials reveals Ford's performance in key indicators in Q1 YoY. Ford Blue revenues (passenger vehicles) account for the biggest share, totaling $21-27 billion in expected revenue, even though Ford lowered its forecast to $21 billion for Q1 2025. Ford Pro (commercial vehicles) range between $15-18 billion in revenue, while Ford Model e (electric vehicles) only account for a small part at $1-1.4 billion quarterly. The total company EBIT's fall was steep from $2.8 billion in the early months of 2024 to just $1.0 billion in Q1 2025. Ford Model e is indeed left unprofitable with negative EBIT across all metrics. EBIT margins dropped substantially from 6.5% to 2.5% across the whole company. Retail units decreased from more than 1 million vehicles in the second to the fourth quarter of 2024 to 971,000 in the first quarter of 2025, suggesting a decrease in demand. Ford Blue saw a particularly big decline from 774,000 to 588,000 units. The data proposes that even though Ford continues to channels high revenues, it is still undergoing poor margin and erosion of profitability.

Consumer's confidence has also decreased, and the index has dropped to 86 in April 2025 from 104.1 in January showing that consumers are not as eager to make major purchases like vehicles. Persistently high interest rates in the US, which have risen to 4.25-4.50% and remain stable at these levels are keeping borrowing costs high, and as a consequence, they are making auto loans more expensive and possibly leading to a decrease in sales. Despite inflation rates falling in April to 2.31%, the broader market expects to see an uptick in inflation rates as well due to tariffs.

Raw Materials' Cost Increases

Steel and aluminum are basic vehicles' manufacturing materials, and Trump's tariffs, like the 25% on steel and 10% on aluminum, directly impact Ford's production cost. A 25% increase in the steel prices can raise the cost per car by hundreds of dollars, all while Ford is operating in its tightest profit margins so far. The car industry is one of the most competitive areas of the economy; hence the task of either absorbing these costs themselves or passing them on to consumers without collapsing their profitability seems like a really tough balance.

Strategic Product Emphasis

Ford has made a switch and is now manufacturing high-margin vehicles that are made in America such as F-150 Lightning and the Mustang Mach-E. F-150 Lightning was able to make 200,000 reservations until Q1 2024, and the revenue generated by this model was $10 billion, while the Mustang Mach-E model accounted for $4.5 billion. Together, they represent 30% of Ford's total annual sales of 2.1 million cars and the cars boast a 25% profit margin - a stark contrast to the 10% profit margin of the discontinued sedans. This step has led to a 7% increase in Ford's total profitability since 2023, notwithstanding the tariff pressures.

Taking Advantage of A Subsidy

Ford has made the most of the U.S. incentives given that it managed to achieve a $500 million tax credit under the Inflation Reduction Act for electric vehicle production and renewables energy upgrades. Along with that, the Advanced Technology Vehicles Manufacturing loan program contributed to the process with $5.9 billion in low-interest loans at a mere 2.5% rate as opposed to market rates of 5% which has saved Ford $150 million on a yearly basis. These funds have resulted in a 10% cut in production of electric vehicles noteworthy of $3,000 per vehicle only counting 2024 data.

Capacity Utilization Maximization

Ford is paying attention to the way it folds these fixed costs; this involves a significant number of units, which means optimization of production capacity is a must. In 2024, Ford posted an increase in the plant utilization rate, as it got a plant from 75% to 85% which moved up vehicle production from 1.9 million to 2.1 million units. This adjustment decreased per vehicle fixed costs by 8%, which now are not $7,000 but rather $6,440. For instance, the Dearborn Truck Plant, which serves in the production of key F-150, is now operating at 90% capacity and this results in a truck fixed cost decrease by 5% per unit.

Recommendation

Ford's supply chain and manufacturing strategies which are fundamentally based on domestic production, vertical integration, as well as strategic sourcing are the main measures against Trump's tariffs. Facing a $1.5 billion yearly tariff burden while others contend it with $2-$2.4 billion, the company becomes able to maintain its pricing, take its fair percentage of the market, and also allocate more funds into development. For these reasons, I rate Ford a Buy.

data.title: PotlatchDeltic: Why We Bought A Position
data.description:
On our last coverage of PotlatchDeltic Corporation (NASDAQ:PCH), we gave it the cold shoulder and suggested investors look to short if the occasion called for it.

We generally require to this to be a little closer to NAV, to hit the Sell rating. Our other tells also suggest we might get a last little run-up here (perhaps with more tariff threats on Canadian timber?) into mid-December. So, weighing all this information gets us to a "Hold" rating, which is still a downgrade from our "Buy". Since we are unlikely to update this story again quickly, we can tell our readers that we would move to a "Sell/Short Sell" if we get $48-$50 in the next couple of weeks.

Source: Great Point To Exit This REIT

PCH did not go anywhere close to the $48-$50 range in December but it did hit it in March (52 week high $48.12). If you shorted then, you made out like a bandit.

The stock is also down 12% from our exact article date.

So the move from buy to hold also worked out well. We go over why we started a defensive long position in the stock and why we are still maintaining a "hold" rating.

A Solid Margin Of Safety

REITs trade in a limited band, at least relative to their NAVs. Sure, you have euphoric moments during ZIRP where many REITs went as high as a 2X multiple on already inflated NAVs. But generally, if you trade around the NAV the discount/premium using mean reversion, you can make money. This logic is even stronger in the case of Timber REITs, which tend to hold closer to their NAVs. As we had compelling evidence that the sector was peaking, we were happy to back off from the buy rating when it was trading near 0.80X (December)-0.85X (March) NAV. But at 0.65X? That is a different story.

On a very long term basis, it is fairly rare to ever get the chance to buy PCH below 0.60X NAV.

So 0.65X, which was what we saw when we placed our trade, things were looking fairly appealing.

We could see a similar bargain proposition in PCH's dividend yield, which was getting well into the attractive zone.

Smart Money Getting Onboard

Those that have followed our earlier work on PCH must have seen how we use the positioning in lumber futures to fine tune our entry and exit points. This involves seeing the net long positioning of the "Commercial" traders on CME. These are the hedgers and include actual consumers and sellers of lumber/timber. They understand the market far better than the "Non-Commercial" block. Generally, we require the Commercials to have the same bias as our trade direction. We had it at the time (April 22, 2025 COT report) and we still do (May 20 COT report shown below).

The commercial traders were net long and the spread was fairly large relative to history. This is good (though not great) positioning and offers a solid chance of making money in the medium term.

Options Provided Impressive Returns For Extremely Low Risk

While the setup was good, it seemed to be lacking the yield and the margin of safety we usually crave on such trades. Fortunately, as is usually the case, options came to the rescue. A covered call on PCH offered a tantalizing 13.41% annualized yield. Most stocks that yield double digits tend to be giant traps and create very poor total returns. A covered call on a quality play, at the right time, actually creates great returns with an improved margin of safety. As can be seen in the case below, by selling an "in-the-money" covered call, we reduced our effective entry price to just $33.88.

We had pointed out earlier that it is almost impossible to buy PCH under 0.6X NAV, but we just did that using options. The current estimate for PCH's NAV is $57 and 0.6X times that is $34.2. Our effective entry price is $33.80, even below that number.

Verdict

We don't expect PCH to be in our portfolio come November. We think it will hold over $35 and get called away. But if it doesn't and certainly anything is possible, we will get long at one of the juiciest prices the stock has ever seen. We will also likely get a chance to the sell the $35 covered call once again at a nice premium as well. At these broader equity market valuations, we rather "rent" quality stocks for high double digit yields rather than buy and hold chronic equity destroyers for yield. PCH certainly fits the former description. As far as the official rating goes, we would rate this a "hold". We would only be interested in slapping a "buy" rating if we see a sub $35 price.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are not definitely written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

data.title: Admiral Group: Still A Solid Quality Growth Pick
data.description:
It's getting on for two years since I last shared my thoughts on U.K. motor insurer Admiral Group (OTCPK:AMIGY) (OTCPK:AMIGF). The bull case was relatively simple. At the time, the company was still emerging from a macro-driven trough in its earnings, driven by the post-COVID normalization of claims frequency as well as the impact of high inflation on claims severity. A then-20x EPS multiple, based on heavily depressed earnings, looked like a very reasonable price to pay.

Thankfully, things have largely played out as I had hoped. Admiral's underwriting profitability and group-wide earnings have indeed rebounded sharply – and that has driven a very nice performance for the shares, with the ADSs returning around 65% in that time, helped a little by a weaker U.S. dollar.

This run leaves Admiral trading for roughly 15.5x 2024 earnings per share. While that may look like a fair multiple for a mature-market motor insurer, Admiral's high-quality earnings and share-driven growth make it cheaper than it appears on paper. Accordingly, I remain bullish.

Back To What It Does Best

Admiral generates the vast majority of its earnings through its U.K. motor insurance business. When I last covered Admiral, this business was still playing catch-up to the macro dynamics that had hit its earnings after COVID. For one, claims frequency had been returning to normal levels after falling due to the lockdowns (fewer cars on the road means fewer accidents). At the same time, the surge in inflation had driven up the cost of said claims, crimping the company's underwriting profitability.

In response to these dynamics, the U.K. car insurance market has seen aggressive price increases. Because this is largely a commoditized business (consumers are typically only motivated by price), premiums tend to lag inflation, as businesses are wary of the impact of price hikes on volumes. The lag period can lead to softer earnings, which is exactly where Admiral was at when I last covered it.

Now, Admiral is an exceptional underwriter. It has a strong record of reporting a comfortable sub-100% combined ratio, meaning it takes in more from customer premiums than it pays out in claims and its own operating costs. While its earnings had been squeezed, it is testament to the quality of its business that it still earned a 29% group-wide return on equity ('ROE') in its worst year (2022).

Positively, these issues have now worked their way through Admiral's financials. As a result, the company is back to doing what it does best: gaining market share while reporting exceptional levels of underwriting profitability. Like many non-U.S. companies, Admiral only provides comprehensive results twice a year, meaning 2024 figures are the latest we have. Last year, the company earned £839 million in pre-tax profit, up 47% on 2023 and over 130% on 2022. In the key U.K. motor segment, pre-tax profit was £955 million, up 61% on 2023 and 82% above 2022. Given higher premiums, underwriting profitability has improved markedly. In the U.K. motor segment, Admiral's core combined ratio was 74.7% last year, around 15ppt lower on my last piece (lower being better for this metric).

When I last covered Admiral after H1 2023 results, aggressive price hikes had caused its volumes to dip slightly. It had 4.76 million vehicles insured in its U.K. motor segment back then, down from a then-peak of 5.14 million in H1 2022. Despite significant price rises, the company's insured base has gone on to rise to 5.69 million vehicles, up almost 20% from my last update, implying Admiral has been gaining market share.

Admiral's track record of winning share is a key component of the bull case. It can simply price risk much better than its competitors can. As a result, not only is Admiral typically very competitive on price, leading to market share gains, but it does so while achieving an exceptional level of underwriting profitability, as shown below.

Since there is a lag between collecting revenue from customers and paying out claims, Admiral also earns money by investing premiums into assets like bonds. Not just that, but because it earns an underwriting profit, it is essentially being paid to invest this money. This is a huge tailwind to its ROE, and more so in a higher interest rate environment, which has been a boon for investment income. Last year, Admiral's underlying investment income yield was 4.0%, up 70bps on 2023 and 240bps higher than 2022. As a result, its investment income has surged three-fold in this period. The company stated that current purchases were being laid on at an average yield of 4.5%, so this tailwind still has a little room to run, albeit higher rates have now largely fed through to this line.

Given these trends, Admiral's group-wide ROE has returned to exceptionally high levels. Last year, it delivered a 56% ROE, up 20ppt on 2023 and 27ppt on 2022.

Alongside its history of market share-driven growth, Admiral's high ROE is the other reason I continue to view it favorably. Specifically, because Admiral earns such a high ROE, it doesn't need to retain much of its earnings in order to fund its growth. As a result, its stated dividend payout ratio of 65% is typically bolstered significantly by special dividends. For example, Admiral's normal dividend was £1.427 per share in respect of 2024, equal to 65.9% of 2024 EPS (£2.166). Special payouts, however, took the total dividend to £1.92 per share, mapping to a payout ratio of roughly 90%. Per the graph below, this is more typical of Admiral's actual payout potential.

This also puts Admiral's current valuation into clearer perspective. At £33.58 in London trading ($45.72 per ADS), these shares trade for 15.5x 2024 EPS. Since Admiral can pay out nearly all of its earnings to shareholders, the starting yield is pretty high, being around 5.7% based on the 2024 dividend. Given natural inflation and its history of gaining market share, Admiral can comfortably deliver the extra 4.3% needed to push its annualized return up to the double-digit mark.

Conclusion

With pricing catching up with inflation and volumes also back to growing, Admiral's earnings have rebounded as expected. While that has driven a very welcome total return since my last piece, these shares remain reasonably valued at less than 16x 2024 EPS. Admiral's high-quality earnings allow it to distribute most of its profits to shareholders, and alongside a strong record of gaining market share, this can lead to double-digit annualized returns for investors. Accordingly, I remain bullish.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: British American Tobacco Outshines Philip Morris As A Dividend Buy
data.description:
BTI vs. PM stock: Previous thesis and new developments

I last wrote on British American Tobacco (NYSE:BTI) in April and Philip Morris International (NYSE:PM) in March. My last BTI article was titled "British American Tobacco Needs Time To Consolidate (Technical Analysis)," and the PM article was titled "Philip Morris: Wall Street Buy Rating Vs. Insider Sales." Both articles were oriented toward the near term. I rated BTI as a hold based on the technical trading patterns at that time, and PM as a sell based on the latest disclosure of insider transactions.

Since then, a few new developments have evolved, both in terms of their own business fundamentals and also in terms of macroscopic geopolitics. Catalysts in the first bucket included the business updates outlined in their earnings reports and investor presentations released after those articles. As for catalysts in the second bucket, the tariff drama that has started on April 2 is the most important one. At this point, I don’t believe there's sufficient information to make any intelligent projections of the outcome of trade negotiations, let alone the potential impact of the outcome on either BTI or PM.

The above uncertainties motivated me to look past the near-term issues and examine both stocks from a long-term perspective. In particular, this article will examine both stocks via the window of their latest dividend declarations due to a few considerations. First, dividends are an important factor for many investors’ interests in BTI and PM to start with. Second, given the long track record of dividend at both companies, I consider the payout as a more accurate metric for their true economic earnings. And finally, perhaps most importantly, past experiences have taught me that the more uncertain the time, the more I should focus on the variables that have the least ambiguity. Dividends are certainly one of the few metrics that involve no degree of subjective interpretation and/or ambiguity.

In the rest of this article, I will assess BTI and PM’s latest fundamentals based on their dividends. The assessment will involve a few perspectives that are not part of the routine check of many dividend investors (such as share repurchases and/or margins) as you can see from my following scorecard. After a comprehensive comparison, my verdict is that BTI offers a much more attractive return/risk ratio than PM, leading me to maintain my sell rating on PM while adjusting my rating on BTI to buy.

1. BTI vs. PM stock: Dividend record

The subsequent assessment will follow an approach that I’ve used in the past to screen dividend growth stocks. The approach is a collection of criteria gathered from my reading of Peter Lynch, which have been detailed in an earlier article. To keep this article within a reasonable length, I will minimize the background of the approach and only quotes what’s absolutely needed as we go.

The approach starts, of course, with establishing a track record as payouts. Lynch’s rule of thumb is a track record of stable payouts for ~20 years or more, which shows the business’ staying power through different economic cycles. Both BTI and PM stocks satisfy these requirements, in my view. Although two notes are worth pointing out. First, Philip Morris only became an independent publicly held company when it was spun off from the parent company, Altria Group, in 2008. Thus, many websites (like the screenshot provided below) only quote its dividend historical since then. Second, BTI’s dividends are declared in British pounds, but the growth reported by many websites is computed in US dollars. Thus, the exchange rates fluctuations could distort its growth rates (and also years of consecutive growth).

With the above, I consider both companies satisfy the first requirement and assign 1 point to each for this round.

2. BTI vs. PEF stock: Valuation and PEGY ratios

As for valuation, the simple P/E ratio can provide misleading information, or at least partial information for dividend stocks for the insights Lynch provided below:

PEG refers to the price-to-earnings growth ratio. He also introduced a modified version of the PEG ratio for dividends stocks. The modified ratio is called the PEGY ratio and is computed as the P/E ratio divided by the sum of the earnings growth rate and dividend yield. The concept is simple yet effective. If the dividends are high enough, investors should not expect (and do not need either) a high growth rate anymore. Just like the PEG ratio, Lynch favors PEGY ratios below 1x.

As such, the table below summarizes my calculation of the PEGY ratios for BTI and PM. These results were based on a few inputs. First, for growth rates, I use BTI and PM’s dividend growth rate in the past three years, which are 2.08% for BTI and 2.62% for PM to approximate the growth rate of their owners’ earnings. For their FWD dividend yield, I calculated it based on their latest payout declarations at the price of this writing, which are $1.35 per share for PM and $0.7491 per share for BTI.

Based on these inputs, PM’s PEGY ratio is 4.24, far above the 1x ideal pick. In contrast, BTI’s PEGY ratio is only 1.12x, quite close to the ideal level. As such, BTI is the undisputed leader on this front.

3. BTI vs. PM: Dividend safety

Dividend safety needs little emphasis for all dividend investors. As seen from the table shown in the forgoing section, PM’s current dividend payout ratio is around 79.4%, which is quite consistent with its historical average of around 80% (which again echoes with my earlier comment about the importance of dividends for PM investors). As for BTI, my estimate for its 2025 payout ratio is about 64.4%, which is also consistent with its historical average level of around 63%. This estimate is based on an FWD payout of $0.7491 per BTI share as just mentioned and an FWD EPS projection of $4.65 per share.

Given that BTI’s payout ratio is noticeably lower than PM both in terms of historical averages and also current levels, I assign another point to BTI in this round.

4. BTI vs. PM stock: Share repurchases

Many (or most) dividend investors invest in dividend stocks for the cash distribution, and share buybacks often do not get their attention. However, this is an equally important aspect because:

Share buybacks are just as valuable for total returns as cash dividends (even more so if you consider tax implications). Yet, it is overlooked by many dividend investors. Plus, share buybacks are a strong indicator of financial strength (that a company has excess cash flow beyond capital expenditures and dividend obligations) and also management's confidence in their own business.

In the case of BTI and PM, BTI is just a much more consistent and larger buyer of its own shares than PM, as you can tell from the next two figures. To wit, BTI has been consistently buying back its shares in recent years. According to its latest cash flow statement (see the second figure below), it spent more than $208 million toward shares repurchases in the last quarter of 2024 alone. In contrast, PM had stopped its repurchasing activities in recent years.

As such, I believe BTI is also the winner on this front.

5. BTI vs. PM stock: Profit margins

Profitability metrics are not part of the analysis for many dividend investors, as they are not directly related to dividend payouts. However, better profit margins offer a thicker buffer against uncertainties. I think profitability metrics have heightened relevance now given the trade conflicts we are experiencing. No matter how these uncertainties play out, the stock with the thicker margin tends to weather better, which in turn translates into an better dividend safety and dividend growth potential.

In the case of BTI and PM, the good news is that both feature superb profitability, as you can see from the following statistics. Both of them boast very high gross profit margin (GPM). PM has a GPM of 65.68%, and BTI's GPM is even higher at 82.33%. As we move downstream in their income statement, BTI and PM show a very similar EBIT Margin of around 37%. Finally, in terms of net margin, PM’s reading of 19.8% is better than BTI’s 11.8%.

All told, I call this around a tie and assign one point to each as I consider that A) both their margins are terrific under a broader scheme of things, and B) their margins are also quite comparable to each other.

Other risks and final thoughts

To conclude, the goal of this reexamination on BTI and PM are motivated by their latest financial reports in their earnings reports and also the developments of the trade uncertainties. As mentioned, I consider the impacts of the trade uncertainties unknowable at this point, and thus urge investors to focus on the return vectors with the least amount of ambiguity, such as dividends. After a holistic comparison, my final scorecard (pasted again below) points to BTI as a better-rounded package featuring high yield, better valuation, lower payout, and strong profitability metrics.

In terms of downside risks, both PM and BTI face largely the same set of generic risks, such as the secular demand decline for their smokable products, regulatory risks, etc. Risks particular to BTI include the uncertain path ahead of its New Categories division. On the positive side, the demand for its modern oral products like Velo has been quite robust lately. However, on the negative side, the profits from these new products are still a relatively minor profit stream for the company overall. The recent rise of illicit single-use vapor products is another risk worth monitoring and will probably continue to hurt BIT until more effective enforcement can tackle illicit products.

Overall, the consumer staple sector (where both PM and BTI belong) is neutrally valued in our model. To wit, the screenshot below is taken from our market dashboard, a tool our Investing Group Service (Envision Early Retirement) developed to obtain a macroscopic view of the market (it’s downloadable as a Google sheet). As seen, the staple sector, approximated by XLP, current sits squarely in the mid of various market sectors when ranked by the dividend yield Z-score. We hope this overview better contextualizes this article. When a whole sector is attractively valued, it is often better – and simpler – to just buy the sector in our experience. But in this case, it's not and thus investors do have to examine the individual stocks more closely and our examination reveals BTI as a strong dividend stock than PM.
